Minet bases his study on the claim that the term carmen is ambiguous because it is found at the intersection of magical and poetical vocabularies and attempts to demonstrate that Vergil both provides a representation of magic as poetry and employs a more conventional metaphor of poetry as magic. Rhodes, Kendal, and Wilson edit fifty-six selections from the writings of translators in sixteenth- and early seventeenth-century England, each presented with a contextual introduction, notes, and suggestions for further reading. Barnard explains (131): "John Dryden's translation of The Works of Virgil [was] the first example of a major literary work by a living English writer published by subscription. … Imposed as a folio in fours, the original publication plans called for two classes of subscribers. The first subscribers, who numbered 101 and paid five guineas apiece in two installments, were to have a fine-paper copy with a special inserted plate dedicated to each of them. … The second subscribers, whose number was unlimited, were to pay two guineas, also in installments, and receive a small-paper copy. In addition to £200 in copy money paid in stages, Dryden was to benefit from a portion of the subscription fees and any gifts from patrons. The translation was thus poised between a publication based on patronage and a commercial venture." Barnard contextualizes the publication by analyzing its printing history in light of the political struggle between Dryden and his publisher. Cobbold gives a prose translation of the Aeneid with illustrations and appendices. Corsalini writes about the translation of Aeneid 2 into Italian by Giacomo Leopardi (1798-1837). Fo considers the difficulties facing a translator of the Aeneid and includes, in appendix (359-62), a "Tabella sulle traduzioni virgiliane (1500-1979)" by Ida Paoloni. García Jurado writes about the ideas of Jorge Luis Borges (1899-1986) concerning the protean character of literature and the phenomenon of translation. Barnard explains (131): "John Dryden's translation of The Works of
Virgil [was] the first example of a major literary work by a living English
writer published by subscription. … Imposed as a folio in fours, the original
publication plans called for two classes of subscribers. The first subscribers,
who numbered 101 and paid five guineas apiece in two installments, were
to have a fine-paper copy with a special inserted plate dedicated to each
of them. … The second subscribers, whose number was unlimited, were
to pay two guineas, also in installments, and receive a small-paper copy.
In addition to £200 in copy money paid in stages, Dryden was to benefit
from a portion of the subscription fees and any gifts from patrons. The
translation was thus poised between a publication based on patronage and
a commercial venture." Barnard contextualizes the publication by analyzing
its printing history in light of the political struggle between Dryden and his
publisher. Cobbold gives a prose translation of the Aeneid with illustrations
and appendices. Corsalini writes about the translation of Aeneid 2 into

plough at Georg. 1.173-74 may be understood in light of his interest in
learned pseudo-etymologies: in his use of the word fagus (beech) he is
aware of Greek φηγȩȢ (oak), and the wood recommended here may well
be the oak. Vergil may have drawn on scholia to Hesiod. Dinter takes the
theoretical approach of intermediality to provide a framework for exploring
the blurring of inscriptional and narrative modes in Latin poetry. He focuses
on instances in the Aeneid of the expression tu/te quoque (translated from

the Greek epigraphic formula καὶ σὺ), which bears sepulchral connotations
and foreshadows death. Minchin compares Homer's approach to storytelling
in the Iliad with Vergil's in the Aeneid, observing that the latter was a written
poem presented first to a listening audience and subsequently to an audience
of readers. Molero Alcaraz studies the uses of the noun fama in the works of
Vergil when it is used in the sense of what is said collectively about someone
or something. Short defines the word sermo in imagistic terms in order to
explain its polysemy between monologic and dialogic interpretations and
also its association with Mercury, arguing, with evidence from the Aeneid,
that the symbolic sameness of sermo and Mercury underlies a systematic
slippage of meaning between these signs. Weber writes that the sense in
which recognoscere is used in Aen. 8.721 is unparalleled in verse but common
in prose as a technical term belonging to the specialized vocabulary of
government and law. A similar analysis may be applied to recensere, used

Barnard explains (131): "John Dryden's translation of The Works of Virgil [was] the first example of a major literary work by a living English writer published by subscription. … Imposed as a folio in fours, the original publication plans called for two classes of subscribers. The first subscribers, who numbered 101 and paid five guineas apiece in two installments, were to have a fine-paper copy with a special inserted plate dedicated to each of them. … The second subscribers, whose number was unlimited, were to pay two guineas, also in installments, and receive a small-paper copy. In addition to £200 in copy money paid in stages, Dryden was to benefit from a portion of the subscription fees and any gifts from patrons. The translation was thus poised between a publication based on patronage and a commercial venture." Barnard contextualizes the publication by analyzing its printing history in light of the political struggle between Dryden and his publisher. Carlson writes about the literary rivalry between John Gower (ca. 1330- 1408) and Geoffrey Chaucer (ca. 1340-1400), observing that "Gower's rivalry with Chaucer was only part of his manifold literary antagonism, however, encompassing not only a senior poet's rivalry with a junior, but also Gower's rivalry with an ancient author, whom Chaucer too used but Gower knew better, Ovid" (932). In his conception of "vaga Fama" at Visio Anglie 1231-32, however, Gower refers to Aen. 4.173-96, "when generally otherwise he is Ovidian, in reply to Chaucer's uncharacteristic usage, preferring Ovid [when he constructs his image of Fama in House of Fame, 713-20] where more often he used Virgil" (951). Christ discusses Heinrich von Veldeke's Eneit (ca. 1175), written in a Dutch literary dialect; its model, the anonymous Old French Roman d'Énéas (ca. 1160); and Vergil's Aeneid. Cornelius situates the moral and political thought of John Gower's Visio Anglie (1381) within its Vergilian intertext. Deist compares Vergil's imagery with that of the Roman d'Énéas. Eitel writes about the influence of the Eclogues on Dante and on his contemporary Giovanni del Virgilio (d. 1319). Mordeglia analyzes a tenth-century allegorical poem that alludes to a number of classical authors through a late-antique and medieval poetic filter; the allusions include forty-four citations of Vergil, with a directness of citation that suggests a parodic treatment. Nazzaro writes about the development of the Christian interpretation of Vergil, especially of the fourth eclogue, in the patristic and medieval worlds, with attention to Lactantius, Constantine, Augustine, Quodvultdeus, and Dante. Wingfield's book contains a chapter 178 - Shirley Werner on "Gavin Douglas' Eneados" (150-77). The Scottish poet Gavin Douglas (ca. 1476-1522) produced the first complete translation of the Aeneid into English (1513). Alarcos Martínez, Miguel. Clément-Tarantino discusses passages in Servius. Delvigo, "'Mythici'
vs 'physici'," examines the relationship of physics and cosmology to myth
in Servius's commentary on the Aeneid, drawing comparative evidence
from the writings of Augustine. Delvigo, "Servio e l'analogia," discusses
Servius, Georg. 4.219-27. Gioseffi, "Come nasce un commento?," starts
with a passage in Seneca (Dial. 10.9.2-4) as background for the analysis
of the formula id est in Latin prose and in late-antique commentaries to
Vergil, paying particular attention to the work of Tiberius Claudius Donatus
and to the different types of notes introduced with this phrase. Lucarini
offers improvements to the text of a previously missing section of the sixth
book of Donatus's Interpretationes Vergilianae as edited by P. K. Marshall,
Manuscripta 37 (1993) 3-20. The text is preserved in Vatican City, Biblioteca
Apostolica Vaticana, Vat. lat. 8222, fols. 2r-9v. Monno observes that Servius's
remarks at Aen. 1.381 and 3.522 on the use of conscendere and the meaning
of humilis imply elements of a physical theory. Pirovano, "Glosse, 2," studies
the interlinear and marginal glosses in Bern, Burgerbibliothek 165. Ramires
comments on Jeunet-Mancy's Commentaire sur l'Énéide de Virgile: Livre
VI (Paris: Les Belles Lettres, 2012). Squillante Saccone discusses passages
from Donatus. Stok's "Esegesi antiche" focuses on Servius. Stok, "Triumuiri
agris diuidendis," writes about the expropriation of land mentioned by
Vergil in the Eclogues in the context of Servius's claim that a commission
of three (triumviri agris dividendis)-composed of Alfenus Varus, Asinius
Pollio, and Cornelius Gallus-managed these expropriations. Zanker argues
that assumptions we have about expressions of meaning may mislead us
into adopting an interpretation of a phrase common in Servius-"hoc vult
dicere"-as if it were equivalent to "ça veut dire." It is more likely, he writes,
that Vergil is the subject of vult and that hoc serves either as a direct object
or as an ablative of means.

De Gianni observes that Ambrose alludes to "dolum ridens" in Ecl. 6.24. Emberger considers Vergil as a model for Calpurnius Siculus. Galli, "Insidie palesi," considers Hosidius Geta's Medea and the Vergiliocentones minores in the Codex Salmasianus (Paris, Bibliothèque nationale, Lat. 10318). Jakobi writes about the influence of Vergil on the sixth-century epic poet Corippus. Kenens examines the text and reception of the Iudicium coci et pistoris iudice Vulcano (AL 190 Shackleton Bailey), probably composed in the fourth century, which makes parodic reference to Vergilian epic. Klaassen observes that Silius's narrative technique in his Punica was drawn from Vergil: Hannibal appears an antitype of Aeneas, while Scipio is the Roman Aeneas; other characters also assume the role of Aeneas in different ways. McGill discusses the prose passage inserted into the De ecclesia in the Codex Salmasianus and the name "Maro Iunior" claimed by the writer of the cento. O'Hogan, "Intertextual Journey," comments on the many echoes of the Aeneid, notably of passages in which Juno plays a role, in Prudentius, who presents himself, as narrator, as a double of Aeneas. Paolucci, "Per la costituzione del testo dell'Alcesta," continues her discussion of the text preserved in the Codex Salmasianus from line 145. Paolucci, "Rileggendo," discusses a number of difficult passages in the cento Versus ad gratiam Domini, basing her observations on C. Arcidiacono's 2011 edition. Trinacty examines the repetition, by Lucan and Silius Italicus, of a bilingual pun found in the Aeneid. In the epilogue to his edition of selected papers on poetry and history, Woodman observes that the narratives of Latin historians exhibit a fundamentally poetic element. He takes as a methodological case study the relationship between Tacitus, Ann. 15.38-40, on the great fire in Rome under Nero, and Aen. 2, on the destruction of Troy. Barchiesi, Alessandro. CQ 63 (2013): 835-53. Clément-Tarantino discusses passages in Servius. Delvigo, "'Mythici' vs 'physici'," examines the relationship of physics and cosmology to myth in Servius's commentary on the Aeneid, drawing comparative evidence from the writings of Augustine. Delvigo, "Servio e l'analogia," discusses Servius, Georg. 4.219-27. Gioseffi, "Come nasce un commento?," starts with a passage in Seneca (Dial. 10.9.2-4) as background for the analysis of the formula id est in Latin prose and in late-antique commentaries to Vergil, paying particular attention to the work of Tiberius Claudius Donatus and to the different types of notes introduced with this phrase. Lucarini offers improvements to the text of a previously missing section of the sixth book of Donatus's Interpretationes Vergilianae as edited by P. K. Marshall, Manuscripta 37 (1993) 3-20. The text is preserved in Vatican City, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, Vat. lat. 8222, fols. 2r-9v. Monno observes that Servius's remarks at Aen. 1.381 and 3.522 on the use of conscendere and the meaning of humilis imply elements of a physical theory. Pirovano, "Glosse, 2," studies the interlinear and marginal glosses in Bern, Burgerbibliothek 165. Ramires comments on Jeunet-Mancy's Commentaire sur l'Énéide de Virgile: Livre VI (Paris: Les Belles Lettres, 2012). Squillante Saccone discusses passages from Donatus. Stok's "Esegesi antiche" focuses on Servius. Stok, "Triumuiri agris diuidendis," writes about the expropriation of land mentioned by Vergil in the Eclogues in the context of Servius's claim that a commission of three (triumviri agris dividendis)-composed of Alfenus Varus, Asinius Pollio, and Cornelius Gallus-managed these expropriations. Zanker argues that assumptions we have about expressions of meaning may mislead us into adopting an interpretation of a phrase common in Servius-"hoc vult dicere"-as if it were equivalent to "ça veut dire." It is more likely, he writes, that Vergil is the subject of vult and that hoc serves either as a direct object or as an ablative of means. Woodbridge, Suffolk, UK: Boydell and Brewer, 2014. Bretzigheimer's topic is the twelfth-century Ylias, which takes as its models the Ilias Latina, Ovid's Metamorphoses, and the Aeneid. Burnyeat responds to the modern critical assumption that Táin Bó Cúailnge (The Cattle Raid of Cooley) is the medieval Irish equivalent to the Aeneid, and to the late-antique and medieval commonplace that, in his relationship to his Homeric sources, Vergil was as much a compilator as he was an auctor. Carlson writes about the literary rivalry between John Gower (ca. 1330- 1408) and Geoffrey Chaucer (ca. 1340-1400), observing that "Gower's rivalry with Chaucer was only part of his manifold literary antagonism, however, encompassing not only a senior poet's rivalry with a junior, but also Gower's rivalry with an ancient author, whom Chaucer too used but Gower knew better, Ovid" (932). In his conception of "vaga Fama" at Visio Anglie 1231-32, however, Gower refers to Aen. 4.173-96, "when generally otherwise he is Ovidian, in reply to Chaucer's uncharacteristic usage, preferring Ovid [when he constructs his image of Fama in House of Fame, 713-20] where more often he used Virgil" (951). Christ discusses Heinrich von Veldeke's Eneit (ca. 1175), written in a Dutch literary dialect; its model, the anonymous Old French Roman d'Énéas (ca. 1160); and Vergil's Aeneid. Cornelius situates the moral and political thought of John Gower's Visio Anglie (1381) within its Vergilian intertext. Deist compares Vergil's imagery with that of the Roman d'Énéas. Eitel writes about the influence of the Eclogues on Dante and on his contemporary Giovanni del Virgilio (d. 1319). Mordeglia analyzes a tenth-century allegorical poem that alludes to a number of classical authors through a late-antique and medieval poetic filter; the allusions include forty-four citations of Vergil, with a directness of citation that suggests a parodic treatment. Nazzaro writes about the development of the Christian interpretation of Vergil, especially of the fourth eclogue, in the patristic and medieval worlds, with attention to Lactantius, Constantine, Augustine, Quodvultdeus, and Dante. Wingfield's book contains a chapter 178 - Shirley Werner on "Gavin Douglas' Eneados" (150-77). The Scottish poet Gavin Douglas (ca. 1476-1522) produced the first complete translation of the Aeneid into English (1513). Alarcos Martínez, Miguel. Bollettino storico-bibliografico subalpino 113 (2015): 137-46.
Rogerson, Anne. "Vegio's Ascanius: Problems in the Continuation of the Aeneid." CRJ
5 (2013): 106-25.
Edgecombe's notes include discussion of an allusion to the Aeneid in a
play by the English writer John Lyly (ca. 1553 or 1554-1606). Putnam writes
about the influence of Vergil on the ekphrastic technique of Neapolitan
humanist Jacopo Sannazaro (1458-1530) in his De partu virginis. Rogerson
reinterprets Maffeo Vegio's fifteenth-century Supplement to the "Aeneid,"
observing that "Vegio does not, as some contemporaries did, criticize
Aeneas overtly. He does, however, pointedly sideline and diminish the figure
of Aeneas' son, Ascanius, to the extent that his succession to his father's
position becomes doubtful" (106).

the pastoral space in the Russian poet's eclogues" (285).
